coredata.dc:title: Sustainable approach to cobalt recycling: Levulinic acid leaching of waste lithium-ion battery cathode materials
coredata.dc:creator: Gupta, Himanshu
Dhiman, Soniya
Stam, Pascal G.
Chaudhary, Savi
Murugavel, Ramaswamy
Shiju, N. Raveendran
coredata.dc:description: The global consumption of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) has increased significantly due to the widespread application of electronic devices and electric vehicles. These batteries contain valuable metals, among which cobalt is a critical component. As a strategic material, it is desirable to recover cobalt from spent LIBs, thereby reducing reliance on primary resources. This study presents a sustainable recycling method that utilizes levulinic acid, a biomass-derived organic acid, in combination with hydrogen peroxide for the recovery of cobalt, with analysis conducted via UV-Visible spectrophotometry. The process specifically targets cobalt recovery from the cathode material of waste LIBs sourced from mobile phones. Key experimental parameters including acid concentration, solid-to-liquid (S/L) ratio, reaction time and temperature were systematically optimized. Under optimal conditions of S/L ratio of 1:50 and 80 °C, over 92 % of the cobalt was successfully leached. Complete cobalt leaching was observed at an S/L ratio of 1:500, demonstrating the high leaching capability of the system. The results demonstrate that levulinic acid is both an efficient and environmentally benign leaching agent for critical metal recovery. This approach aligns with circular economy principles by utilizing a renewable, waste-derived leachant and offers a promising green pathway for LIB recycling.
coredata.openaccessSponsorName: Dutch institutions: Hybrid, Full OA RAP 2025
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion batteries
Cathode
Cobalt
Leaching
Recycle
Levulinic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Kinetics of cobalt leaching from LIBs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a Various electronic devices consisting of lithium-ion batteries.(AI supportive tools also assisted the preparation of this figure), Fig. 1b different constituents of lithium-ion mobile phone batteries.
Influence of different parameters a) concentration of levulinic acid b) reaction time c) hydrogen peroxide concentration d) temperature on cobalt leaching from spent LIBs.

Shrinking core model a) shrinking of core particle with time, b) liquid film diffusion control mechanism, c) chemical reaction control mechanism, d) ash or product layer diffusion control mechanism (Fig. 3a - inspired by Levenspiel, 1999; Li et al., 2021; Fig. 3b,c,d – prepared using AI supportive tools).

Application of the shrinking core model for different mechanisms of cobalt leaching from waste LIBs using levulinic acid: a) liquid film diffusion control, b) reaction control, and c) solid product diffusion control mechanism. Note that R
2 is the correlation coefficient and is distinct from R2.

a Process flowsheet for cobalt recovery from spent LIB cathode material using levulinic acid leaching and oxalate precipitation.Fig. 5b Schematic representation of cobalt recovery from spent LIBs via leaching with biomass-derived levulinic acid, followed by selective precipitation as cobalt oxalate.
a XRD pattern of recovered cobalt oxalate from waste LIBs.Fig. 6 b FE-SEM image of recovered cobalt oxalate from waste LIBs.
Concentration of metals in cathode material of waste LIBs.
Percentage leaching of cobalt at varying S/L ratio.

head.ce:title: Sustainable approach to cobalt recycling: Levulinic acid leaching of waste lithium-ion battery cathode materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Bombay, Powai, Mumbai-400076, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The global consumption of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) has increased significantly due to the widespread application of electronic devices and electric vehicles. These batteries contain valuable metals, among which cobalt is a critical component. As a strategic material, it is desirable to recover cobalt from spent LIBs, thereby reducing reliance on primary resources. This study presents a sustainable recycling method that utilizes levulinic acid, a biomass-derived organic acid, in combination with hydrogen peroxide for the recovery of cobalt, with analysis conducted via UV-Visible spectrophotometry. The process specifically targets cobalt recovery from the cathode material of waste LIBs sourced from mobile phones. Key experimental parameters including acid concentration, solid-to-liquid (S/L) ratio, reaction time and temperature were systematically optimized. Under optimal conditions of S/L ratio of 1:50 and 80 °C, over 92 % of the cobalt was successfully leached. Complete cobalt leaching was observed at an S/L ratio of 1:500, demonstrating the high leaching capability of the system. The results demonstrate that levulinic acid is both an efficient and environmentally benign leaching agent for critical metal recovery. This approach aligns with circular economy principles by utilizing a renewable, waste-derived leachant and offers a promising green pathway for LIB recycling.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Modern energy storage devices, including mobile phones, personal computers, laptops, cameras, and especially electric vehicles, primarily rely on lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) ( Fig. 1a). LIBs are majorly employed due to their light weight, wider operating temperature range, rechargeability, and high energy density (Chavan et al., 2024). Global LIB demand continues to grow annually, with a rapid increase in electronic devices and electric mobility. Consequently, a significant surge is also being observed in the number of LIBs approaching their end-of-life.

Globally, the LIB market was valued at nearly USD 117.8 billion in 2024 and is estimated to grow at a 13.5 % compound annual growth rate (CAGR) through 2029 (Arora, 2025). By the end of 2025, global output of spent LIBs is expected to reach about 1.36 million tons (Gao et al., 2023). In 2021 alone, approximately 1.4 billion mobile phones were sold. Interestingly, nearly 7 billion of the world’s 8 billion people currently own a smartphone (Kogler et al., 2024). The growing demand for advanced features such as improved camera quality and high-speed 4 G/5 G connectivity has accelerated device turnover, leading to shorter life cycles for LIB-powered electronics. Consequently, the volume of end-of-life LIBs is anticipated to reach around 54 million metric tons by 2050 (Pol, 2025). The rapid accumulation of waste LIBs presents serious environmental risks, making their disposal and sustainable management a major global challenge.

Waste LIBs contain a complex mixture of toxic and valuable critical metals, notably lithium and cobalt. Structurally, LIBs are composed of three primary components: cathode, anode, and an olefinic separator (Fig. 1b). The cathode is the principal source of critical metals, particularly lithium and cobalt, which are coated onto aluminium foil current collectors. In contrast, the anode contains primarily graphite deposited on copper foil and contains only trace amounts of cobalt. The separator, which physically separates the cathode and anode to prevent short-circuiting, is typically a microporous membrane made of polymeric materials such as polypropylene (PP) and polyethylene (PE) (Gupta and Shiju, 2025; Agarwal et al., 2024). Given the strategic importance and scarcity of metals like cobalt and lithium, the recycling and recovery of cathode materials from waste LIBs are crucial for promoting resource sustainability and reducing the environmental burden associated with battery waste. We have previously reported the chemical recycling of waste plastics through liquefaction (Boel et al., 2024; Chien et al., 2025).

Currently, a major part of LIBs is being landfilled, causing soil contamination due to the presence of metals, and leaching of these metals causes water contamination (Mrozik et al., 2021). Electrolytes and heavy metals release threatens ecological as well as environmental sustainability. Presently, global recycling of spent LIBs is only 5 % (Duvedi et al., 2024). Australia and United States started initiatives to enhance the recycling capacity for LIBs (Collis et al., 2023). China is leading in recycling LIBs across the globe. As per reports, there are around 50 leading LIB recycling enterprises that exist across the globe, among them 30 have their headquarters in China (Miao et al., 2022). The European Commission proposal 2020 suggests the incorporation of a minimum of 12 % of cobalt in new batteries by 2030, which will be further enhanced to 20 % in 2035 (Hoarau and Lorang, 2022). Despite various metals, lithium and cobalt are the most important and valuable metals to be recovered during the recycling of LIBs. By the end of 2025, as per reports, > 50 % of global reserves for cobalt will be consumed by the LIB industry alone (Iqbal et al., 2022), and by 2030, it will consume around 80 % (Roelfsema et al., 2025). Therefore, cobalt recycling from waste LIBs is of paramount importance. The lithium cobalt oxide (LCO) type of batteries consists of a high quantity of critical raw metal cobalt, which makes the LCO batteries highly suitable secondary resource for the recovery of cobalt. The demand for consumer electronics like smartphones, laptops, and tablets, as well as high-quality recycled cobalt, is continuously increasing for its reutilization in different fields, including re-entry into the battery manufacturing supply chain for sustainability and economic viability. The simpler formulation of the LCO battery requires fewer downstream steps than other complex recycling materials, thus reducing capital and operating expenditure during the separation process. Various methods for recycling metals from LIBs as well as other matrices employing hydrometallurgy and pyrometallurgy have been reported, but these are limited to a certain extent. The pyrometallurgical methods are disadvantageous due to application of high temperature and achieving partial separation, requiring further refining and processing. In contrast, hydrometallurgical methods involve lower consumption of energy and gives better selectivity (Dhiman et al., 2024; Kumar et al., 2023; Meshram et al., 2014; Jha et al., 2013). Different studies focused on the recycling of cobalt using inorganic acids are reported (Urias et al., 2020; Medić et al., 2023; Pinna et al., 2017), but the utilization of acids is not an eco-friendly method and is hazardous to the environment. Therefore, studies for the recovery of cobalt using organic acids are required, which are economical and eco-friendly. A few studies on the application of organic acids are also reported (Xu et al., 2021; Sethurajan et al., 2022; Li et al., 2024; Cerrillo-Gonzalez et al., 2024), but the application of levulinic acid from lithium cobalt oxide (LiCoO2) based LIBs for recovery of cobalt as cobalt oxalate is reported for the first time in the present study. Previously, levulinic acid was reported to leach metals from a different feedstock, NMC523. However, the applied method was different and the leaching time was 24 h or higher, and the recovery of cobalt as cobalt oxalate was not achieved and characterized (Jiang et al., 2023). The applied method in the present study is easy, efficient, and less time-consuming, which involves lower energy for better scalability and environmental sustainability. Levulinic acid is a biomass-based five-carbon keto acid known for its strong binding ability to metal and low production cost. It belongs to a broader class of renewable oxygenated platform molecules such as lactates and levulinates, which have attracted considerable attention in green chemistry due to their versatility and sustainable origin (Coşkuner et al., 2017; Gitis et al., 2018; Ramos-Fernandez et al., 2014; Chung et al., 2022; 2024). It can be produced from lignocellulosic biomass via acid-catalyzed dehydration of carbohydrates (Azlan et al., 2022), whereas H2O2 can break down into water. Given its renewable origin and functional properties, the use of levulinic acid for recycling LIBs and recovering cobalt offers a promising alternative to conventional and environmentally hazardous alternatives, including mineral acids such as sulphuric acid (Swain et al., 2007). The high initial dosage of H2O2 acts as a kinetic buffer to ensure a sufficient concentration of the active reducing species remains available throughout the reaction time. The 40 % dosage was required to achieve the required high cobalt recovery using levulinic acid as a leaching agent within a particular time. However, future studies may be conducted to reduce the application of hydrogen peroxide with efficient leaching performance. Furthermore, employing a simple analytical technique such as UV-Visible spectrophotometry for cobalt quantification enhances the practicality and the cost-effectiveness of the process, making it attractive from both environmental and economic perspectives.

This study explores (i) the use of levulinic acid to recover cobalt from the cathode material of spent LIBs sourced from mobile phones. (ii) Key experimental parameters, including the concentration of organic acid, solid-to-liquid (S/L) ratio, reaction time, and temperature, were systematically optimized to maximize cobalt leaching efficiency. (iii) To evaluate kinetics of cobalt leaching from spent LIB cathode material, the shrinking core model (SCM) was employed. (iv) The results highlight the potential of levulinic acid as an effective, biomass-derived leaching agent for critical metal recovery. By utilizing a sustainable reagent obtained from waste, this approach aligns with circular economy principles. Moreover, the developed method is eco-friendly, scalable, and offers an economic solution to enhance the economic viability of battery recycling processes.

Waste mobile phone LIBs were collected from the technology centre of University of Amsterdam, Netherlands. These were dismantled to collect the cathode, separator material and anode. Separated cathode material was kept in an oven at 80 °C for 24 h to remove binders present with the material. The cathode material was ground to a fine powder passing through the sieve of 75 µm. Levulinic acid (>98 %) was supplied by Sigma-Aldrich. Hydrogen peroxide (30 % w/v) was received from Fisher Scientific. Deionized water was used throughout the analysis. Cobalt concentration was examined using a UV-Visible spectrophotometer (Varian, 50 Bio) at λmax of 511 nm. Metal concentrations have been optimized with results obtained from ICP-MS (Agilent 4650) analysis. The recovered cobalt oxalate samples were characterized by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) using a Rigaku, (SmartLab SE) diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5419 Å). Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FE-SEM) and Energy Dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopic information were collected using a Zeiss Sigma 500 field emission scanning electron microscope at an accelerating voltage of 3 kV by smearing the powdered samples onto carbon tape and sputtering with gold before imaging.

The total metal contents in the prepared cathode powder were quantified by digesting 0.1 g of the cathode powder in 5 mL (HNO3/HCl = 1/3) aqua regia at 80 ºC for 4 h in a round-bottom flask under reflux conditions. The obtained solution was centrifuged, and the leach liquor was diluted before analysis with a UV-Visible spectrophotometer for cobalt and with ICP-MS for all metals. The concentrations of various metals in the digested sample are presented as Table 1.

Cathode powder of the LIBs from mobile phones was subjected to varying concentrations of levulinic acid. Cathode sample amount of 0.1 g mixed with 5 mL of levulinic acid containing 40 % hydrogen peroxide and placed in round bottom flask fitted with a reflux condenser. The influence of different parameters, including S/L ratio, concentration, reaction time as well as temperature, was measured. The contents were stirred for the specified time intervals. After the completion of leaching, the liquid was transferred to the centrifuge tube and centrifuged for 20 min at 4000 rpm. Thereafter, the supernatant liquid has been separated and analysed using a UV-Visible spectrophotometer. The analysis for cobalt was performed at a λmax of 511 nm. Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) was employed to analyse concentrations of other metals and to optimize the concentration of cobalt using a UV-Visible spectrophotometer. The leaching percentage of metal ions was evaluated using Eq. (1):
(1) Leaching percentage (% L) = Cs (mg/g)/Cd (mg/g) * 100
Here, Cs (mg/g) is the leached metal amount per 1 g of cathode powder, Cd (mg/g) total metal concentration (mg/g) in 1 g of cathode powder as per aqua regia based digestion experiment.

Results suggest that cobalt is the major component of the cathode material of mobile phone LIBs, with other metals present only in significantly lower concentrations (Table 1). The composition of cathode powder was also confirmed by Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) analysis of the cathode powder (Fig. S1). The FTIR spectrum of cathode powder coincides with the FTIR spectrum of lithium cobalt oxide reported in literature (Hutama et al., 2021), confirming cobalt and lithium as the main constituents of the LIB in the present study. The XRD spectrum of the material also shows similar peak patterns as reported in literature (Medić et al., 2020), but a slight peak shift is observed (Fig. S2). The shift of peaks observed in the XRD spectrum may be due to the loss of lithium ions due to aging (Lahtinen et al., 2021). The lower concentration of lithium observed in Table 1 is attributed to the loss of lithium. Given this composition, cobalt was identified as the primary target for recovery in this study, and the research was accordingly focused on optimizing cobalt extraction from the spent LIB cathode material.

Results indicated that rising concentration of levulinic acid enhanced cobalt leaching, with optimal leaching observed at a concentration of 4 M levulinic acid ( Fig. 2a). Initially, the levulinic acid molecule interacts with cobalt ions to form cobalt levulinate complex. As the concentration of levulinic acid increases, more cobalt ions are complexed, though the availability of free cobalt ions in solution decreases, leading to a gradual increase in complex formation. At 4 M levulinic acid, most cobalt ions are bound to levulinic acid molecules. A previous study on the leaching of cobalt using glycine also demonstrated enhanced cobalt recovery with increasing glycine concentration (Nayaka et al., 2016). Similarly, Peng et al. (2018) investigated that applying a higher sulfuric acid (H2SO4) concentration, in the presence of a constant amount of reducing agent, improved cobalt leaching efficiency, with the optimal concentration range being 2–4 M H2SO4. An increase in hydrochloric acid concentration also led to enhanced cobalt leaching efficiency, reaching up to 94 % (Dhiman and Gupta, 2019).

The results showed that cobalt leaching increased with longer reaction times, reaching an optimum at 4 h, after which the leaching efficiency plateaued (Fig. 2b). This indicates that as reaction time increases, more cobalt ions interact with levulinic acid molecules until equilibrium is achieved at 4 h. A similar trend was reported in a previous study using H2SO4 with ascorbic acid as reducing agent, where cobalt leaching equilibrium was achieved within 1–4 h at different temperatures (Peng et al., 2018). Additionally, an optimum time of 189 min was identified for the recovery of cobalt employing synergistic system of sulphuric and citric acid (Roshanfar et al., 2024).

The results revealed that increasing the amount of H2O2 enhanced the leaching efficiency of cobalt with an optimal recovery of cobalt observed at 40 % amount of hydrogen peroxide (Fig. 2c). The results reveal that hydrogen peroxide acts as reducing agent for Co3 + ions and reduces them to Co2+ ions (Eq. 2), which reacts with levulinic acid giving pink colour to the solution.
(2) LiCoO2 + 3C5H8O3 + H2O2 → Li+ + Co2+ + 3C5H7O3¯ + 2H2O

As H2O2 concentration in solution increases, the reduction of Co³ ⁺ ions to Co²⁺ becomes more efficient, thereby enhancing the leaching of cobalt. The formation of Co²⁺ ions imparts a characteristic pink color to the leachate, which facilitates the spectrophotometric detection of cobalt using UV-Visible spectroscopy in the visible range at a λmax of 511 nm. Studies have shown that the addition of H2O2 as a reducing agent is most effective when introduced at the beginning of the reaction, ensuring maximum leaching (Vieceli et al., 2023). This enhancement in cobalt leaching through the application of H2O2 as a reducing agent was also reported by various researchers employing different acids and experimental conditions (Verma et al., 2021; Cheng et al., 2023; Willner et al., 2024).

It is evident from the results that with the rise in temperature of the solution, the leaching of cobalt is increased, indicating the endothermic nature of the process (Fig. 2d). After 70 °C, the leaching of cobalt becomes constant, suggesting the achievement of equilibrium. Therefore, for further studies, the selected temperature was 80 °C. At 90 °C, the researchers found that leaching efficiency is decreased due to the beginning of volatilization of acid or solvent from the solution (Nayaka et al., 2016). Various researchers also indicated an optimum temperature range of 80 °C for cobalt leaching (Sahu et al., 2024; Ahmed et al., 2025).

The results indicated 100 % leaching of cobalt at an S/L ratio of 1: 500. With a decrease in S/L ratio, the leaching percentage of cobalt decreases due to the presence of an insufficient number of acid molecules to leach cobalt ( Table 2). In the present study, the 1: 50 S/L ratio was utilized to investigate other parameters. A decrease in the leaching of cobalt with rise in S/L ratio was also observed during the leaching of cobalt with tartaric acid, and 1: 100 S/L ratio was considered optimum (Sahu et al., 2024).

In general, the maximum leaching efficiency of 100 % was found at 1: 500 S/L ratio. Also, at S/L ratio of 1: 50, optimum leaching of cobalt (>92 %) was achieved at 4 M levulinic acid, 40 % H2O2, 4 h reaction time and 80 °C temperature.

Cobalt leaching from spent LIB cathodes in a levulinic acid medium primarily depends on the solubility of cobalt ions. In its original state, cobalt exists as Co³ ⁺ in the form of LiCoO2. However, Co³ ⁺ ions do not have incomplete d-orbital due to which it does not give colour to the solution, and the analysis also suggested that it does not form stable species with levulinic acid. However, H2O2 addition serves as a reducing agent, converting Co3+ to Co2+. Unlike Co3+, the Co2+ ions were found to form complexes with levulinic acid, thereby enhancing their dissolution in the leaching medium. As a result, increasing the concentration of H2O2 in the system significantly improves cobalt leaching efficiency (Fig. 2c). This redox-driven complexation pathway underscores the crucial role of H2O2 in improving cobalt solubility. The possible mechanism for the leaching of cobalt in the presence of H2O2 as a reducing agent is given below:
(3) LiCoO2 + 2C5H8O3 + H2O2→Li+ + Co(C5H7O3)2 + O2 + 2H2O

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetics of cobalt leaching from LIBs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the kinetics of cobalt leaching from spent LIB cathode material, the shrinking core model (SCM) was employed. As illustrated in Fig. 3b, cobalt leaching may be distinctly divided into two stages. The initial stage, up to approximately 30 min, is characterized by rapid cobalt dissolution, after which the leaching rate slows considerably as the system approaches equilibrium. To analyze the rate-controlling step during the early stage, three classical SCM equations were considered: liquid film diffusion control, chemical reaction control, and ash or product layer diffusion control (Fig. 3).

These are mathematically represented by Eqs. (4), (5), and (6), respectively (Levenspiel, 1999; Aarabi-Karasgani et al., 2010), where the time ‘t’ is normalised with respect to the total time in complete conversion of the particle, denoted as ‘τ’ and X is the fraction of solid converted into product at time ‘t’. Due to the influence of equilibrium effects, the model assumptions are not valid for extended time intervals beyond 30 min. Therefore, the SCM analysis in this study was restricted to the first 30 min of leaching. This allowed for a clearer assessment of the dominant mechanisms governing cobalt extraction during the kinetically active phase of the process.
(4)
t
τ
=
1 −
( 1 − X )
2 / 3
(5)
t
τ
=
1 −
( 1 − X )
1 / 3
(6)
t
τ
=
1 −
3 ( 1 − X )
2 / 3
+
2 ( 1 − X )

In the present study, the right-hand sides of Eqs. (4), (5), and (6) are considered as R1, R2, and R3, respectively. The plots of R1, R2, and R3 versus time till 30 min at different temperatures are given as Fig. 4a, b & c, respectively.

The values of the correlation coefficient (R
2 ) from the kinetic modelling show that both the liquid film diffusion control and the chemical reaction control models agree well with the experimental data. However, the solid product layer diffusion control model shows poor correlation and is thus not applicable to the system under study. The liquid film diffusion control model shows the best fit, suggesting that the kinetics is primarily governed by diffusion of levulinic acid through the liquid film to the surface of cathode particles during cobalt leaching from spent LIBs. However, the R
2 value for the reaction control model is also relatively high and close to that of the best-fitting model. This implies that the chemical reaction occurring at the core surface is also a significant factor in contributing mechanism for cobalt leaching using levulinic acid. The activation energy evaluated using the Arrhenius equation was found to be 11.2 kJ mol−1. The activation energy is higher than 40 kJ mol−1 for a chemical reaction-controlled process and is lower than 20 kJ mol−1 for a diffusion-controlled process (Faraji et al., 2022). The value of activation energy in the present study revealed that the process is mainly controlled by diffusion, followed by chemical reaction. A previous study on the cobalt leaching with sulphuric acid and hydrogen peroxide from the black mass obtained by LIB incineration indicated the occurrence of a mixed mechanism involving surface layer diffusion as well as chemical reaction control (Wongnaree et al., 2024). However, in another study on cobalt leaching from LIBs using gluconic acid, diffusion through the product layer and chemical reaction are predominant steps (Gerold et al., 2024). This may be ascribed to various other factors like solubility of the formed complex in the medium, porosity, and surface chemistry. The copper leaching from printed circuit boards employing sulphuric acid and H2O2 also suggests the occurrence of mixed control mechanisms (Hao et al., 2022).

The cobalt-containing leachate obtained under the optimum conditions was further utilized to recover cobalt as cobalt oxalate, a valuable intermediate with potential applications in materials synthesis and battery manufacturing. To initiate the precipitation process, the pH of the leachate was carefully maintained at 1.5 by employing a 1 M oxalic acid solution. This pH adjustment is critical, as it facilitates selective precipitation of cobalt while minimizing co-precipitation of impurities. The resulting solution was maintained under constant stirring at 50 °C for 2 h on a magnetic stirrer to ensure homogeneous mixing and complete precipitation. The light pink colour precipitate in the solution was then filtered, washed using deionized water, and dried in an oven at 60 °C. The cobalt recovery process from waste LIBs cathode material is presented as a flowsheet in Fig. 5a. Additionally, Fig. 5b presents a visual summary of the experimental procedure, encompassing both the leaching and precipitation stages for cobalt recovery. This sequential approach highlights the potential of biomass-derived levulinic acid and oxalic acid in developing environmentally friendly and efficient hydrometallurgical processes for the critical metal recovery from electronic waste.

The obtained cobalt oxalate was analysed by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD). The XRD pattern of the sample is presented in Fig. 6a, showing good alignment with the diffraction peaks reported in literature (Ren et al., 2009). The pattern showed seven diffraction peaks at (202), (004), (400), (022), (511), (224), and (602) planes, which are in agreement with the orthorhombic cobalt oxalate dehydrate spectrum (Ren et al., 2009). The morphology of the recovered cobalt oxalate was examined by Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FE-SEM) and Energy Dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy. The FE-SEM image confirms the orthorhombic shape of the recovered cobalt oxalate (Fig. 6b). The EDX spectrum showed only peaks corresponding to cobalt, carbon, and oxygen, indicating the high purity of recovered cobalt oxalate (Fig. S3).

The developed methodology for leaching of cobalt from waste lithium-ion batteries using a waste cellulose-derived organic acid, levulinic acid, followed by cobalt recovery as cobalt oxalate, forms a green hydrometallurgical circuit. The primary application of the process involves the consumption of two waste streams, cellulosic biomass and LCO batteries as well as creating a secure and sustainable supply chain for critical metal cobalt, achieving circular economy. Key experimental parameters, including the concentration of organic acid, solid-to-liquid (S/L) ratio, reaction time, and temperature, were systematically optimized to maximize cobalt leaching efficiency. The recovered material can act as a precursor for utilization in another battery, reducing the high reagent cost. The applied method in the present study is easy, efficient, and less time-consuming, which involves lower energy for better scalability and environmental sustainability. The kinetics of cobalt leaching from spent LIB cathode material is evaluated using the shrinking core model (SCM) using different classical equations, and the activation energy is calculated. However, further research can be done on the challenges associated with the process, such as the decrease of H2O2 concentration, without affecting the leaching and recovery of cobalt. Further investigations can be made utilizing other reducing agents, or electrochemical reduction can be applied to remove the entire reagent consumption. More research is required for the recycling and regeneration of levulinic acid, as well as reutilization for multiple cycles.

This study presents an efficient, simple, low-cost, and environmentally friendly method to recover cobalt from LIBs using biomass-derived levulinic acid, along with spectrophotometric detection. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) serves as effective reducing agent, and increasing its concentration enhances the reduction of Co³ ⁺ to Co²⁺, thereby improving cobalt leaching. Under optimal conditions of 4 M levulinic acid, 40 % H2O2, a solid-to-liquid (S/L) ratio of 1:50, and a temperature of 80 °C, more than 92 % of cobalt was successfully recovered from waste LIBs. The kinetic study employing the shrinking core model suggests diffusion as the main rate-controlling step. Cobalt was subsequently recovered as cobalt oxalate by precipitation using 1 M oxalic acid at pH 1.5. The XRD, FE-SEM, and EDX spectra confirm morphology and high purity for recovered cobalt oxalate. The simplicity, environmental compatibility, and accessibility of the process highlight its potential for further development and scale-up at the industrial level.
This project has received funding from the European Union's Horizon Europe Research and Innovation Programme under Grant Agreement No. 101058540 (PLASTICE Project, https://plastice.eu/).

Savi Chaudhary: Investigation. Ramaswamy Murugavel: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation. Soniya Dhiman: Investigation. Pascal G. Stam: Investigation. Himanshu Gupta: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Raveendran Shiju: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the European Union's Horizon Europe Research and Innovation Programme for funding the PLASTICE project (grant agreement no. 101058540).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.eti.2025.104675.

Direct sonochemical leaching of Li, Co, Ni, and Mn from mixed Li-ion batteries with organic acids. Energies 17(16), 4055. https://doi.org/10.3390/en17164055.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Leaching kinetics of valuable metals from calcined material of spent lithium-ion batteries
